serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai green curry with beef is approximately 450 g, typically presented as a bowl of coconut milk-based curry with sliced beef, bamboo shoots, and Thai basil, served alongside steamed jasmine rice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Curry, beef, with coconut milk (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database for Thai curry dishes
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai green curry with beef exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo as a single composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy beef curry with coconut milk entries and HPB Singapore curry dish composites. The input record values (energy 144 kcal, protein 7.78 g, fat 10.67 g, carbohydrate 4.44 g per 100 g) are consistent with published ranges for Thai green beef curry and have been retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 400 mg/100 g is consistent with fish-sauce-heavy Thai curry preparations documented in USDA and HPB sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on coconut milk curry with low-carbohydrate meat base
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai green curry with beef. GI estimated at approximately 38 (low) based on analogous coconut milk-based curry dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The low GI is driven by the high fat and protein content from coconut milk and beef which slow gastric emptying and glucose absorption. Carbohydrate content is low at 4.44 g/100 g. Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as: (38 × 4.44 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 4.5) / 100 = approximately 7.6, rounded to 7.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request reduced fish sauce, and avoid adding salt or condiments at the table; the saturated fat at 7.78 g/100 g from coconut milk also contributes to arterial stiffness and should be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate at 4.44 g/100 g with estimated GI of 38 (low), but high saturated fat from coconut milk at 7.78 g/100 g may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai green curry with beef is acceptable for Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without jasmine rice or with a reduced portion of brown rice; monitor total saturated fat intake as coconut milk contributes significantly to the daily saturated fat budget, and request reduced palm sugar in the curry paste.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 7.78 g/100 g from coconut milk and beef, sodium at 400 mg/100 g, and trans fat at 0.11 g/100 g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to small portions (150 g or less), request reduced coconut milk and fish sauce, substitute beef with a leaner protein such as chicken breast or tofu, and avoid pairing with high-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; saturated fat at 7.78 g/100 g exceeds the RA threshold of 5 g/100 g; nightshade ingredients (Thai eggplant, pea eggplant, chilli) may trigger flares
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef with chicken or seafood, request reduced coconut milk to lower saturated fat, and avoid versions containing Thai eggplant, pea eggplant, or chilli if nightshade-sensitive.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Green Curry with Beef (แกงเขียวหวานเนื้อ; Chinese: 青咖喱牛肉) is a classic Thai curry dish consisting of tender slices of beef simmered in a rich, aromatic coconut milk-based sauce prepared with green curry paste, bamboo shoots, kaffir lime leaves, lemongrass, galangal, and fresh Thai basil leaves. The dish is finished with fish sauce for umami depth and may include palm sugar for subtle sweetness. The curry has a creamy, slightly spicy, and herbaceous flavour profile with a smooth coconut milk base. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 144 kcal, 7.78 g protein, 10.67 g fat (of which 7.78 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 4.44 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-energy, high-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is elevated at 400 mg/100 g due to fish sauce and curry paste. The estimated glycemic index is low (approximately 38), driven by the high fat and protein content slowing glucose absorption. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension due to sodium load and for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat; it is moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without rice. It is commonly served in Thai restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia as a main course with steamed jasmine rice. A standard restaurant portion is approximately 450 g.

confidence_reason: No exact composite entry for Thai green curry with beef exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy beef curry with coconut milk composites and HPB Thai curry dish entries. The input values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for Thai green curry with beef as a composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore nutrient database as of 2024. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not constitute an approved primary nutrient source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; it was therefore not used as primary evidence. The ethnic_tag 'Pad Thai' in the input has been removed as it is not relevant to this dish. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore; this has been corrected to Thailand as the dish's culinary origin, while noting it is commonly served in Singapore. Trans fat at 0.11 g/100 g is retained from the input as a plausible trace value for a beef and coconut milk preparation. Halal status is marked false as fish sauce (non-halal certified in many preparations) is a primary ingredient; this should be verified against the specific restaurant's certification.
